items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.

items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.

items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.
It's Marketing exaggerating eco-credentials. “Genuine environmental action” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Paying the LIVING WAGE may lead to.
items.correct: Higher labour costs but better retention
items.why: It's Higher labour costs but better retention. “Lower staff motivation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Ethical sourcing raises material cost from £6 to £7.50 per unit. For 8,000 units, what is the EXTRA cost, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 12000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
